coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) synthesized under certain parameters of hydrothermal synthesis are reported to demonstrate ultra-bright luminescence in the blue-violet region with a quantum yield up to ∼100 %. However, the questions remain: is this luminescence really belong to the nanoparticles or to concomitant molecular luminophores; at what stage of the CDs’ synthesis such luminophores are formed and lost; how exactly structure of the reacted precursors changes when the synthesis parameters change? In this study, to answer these questions, the array of 392 samples of ethylenediamine and citric acid aqueous solutions undergone the process of hydrothermal method of synthesis, varying EDA:CA ratio in the range of 0–20:1, temperature in 80–200 °C, and reaction time in 0.5–6 h. For all samples the luminescence excitation-emission matrices, optical absorption and FTIR spectra were obtained, quantum yields and luminophores’ intensity of samples’ luminescence at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm were calculated. Based on the obtained data, the processes of CDs’ gradual synthesis – polymerization, dehydration and carbonization – were identified, the changes in the composition of the reaction products during different stages of synthesis were revealed. It was established that the formation of the main samples’ luminophores starts with the polymerization of precursors, accelerates with the initial carbonization of the samples, while the stage of graphitizing carbonization – formation of CDs cores – brings their partially destruction.

head.ce:title: Toward carbon dots from citric acid and ethylenediamine, part 1: Structure, optical properties, main luminophore at different stages of synthesis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) synthesized under certain parameters of hydrothermal synthesis are reported to demonstrate ultra-bright luminescence in the blue-violet region with a quantum yield up to ∼100 %. However, the questions remain: is this luminescence really belong to the nanoparticles or to concomitant molecular luminophores; at what stage of the CDs’ synthesis such luminophores are formed and lost; how exactly structure of the reacted precursors changes when the synthesis parameters change? In this study, to answer these questions, the array of 392 samples of ethylenediamine and citric acid aqueous solutions undergone the process of hydrothermal method of synthesis, varying EDA:CA ratio in the range of 0–20:1, temperature in 80–200 °C, and reaction time in 0.5–6 h. For all samples the luminescence excitation-emission matrices, optical absorption and FTIR spectra were obtained, quantum yields and luminophores’ intensity of samples’ luminescence at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm were calculated. Based on the obtained data, the processes of CDs’ gradual synthesis – polymerization, dehydration and carbonization – were identified, the changes in the composition of the reaction products during different stages of synthesis were revealed. It was established that the formation of the main samples’ luminophores starts with the polymerization of precursors, accelerates with the initial carbonization of the samples, while the stage of graphitizing carbonization – formation of CDs cores – brings their partially destruction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a wide class of carbon nanomaterials with a characteristic size of about 10 nm, which have gained interest due to their luminescence properties [1–6]. The discovery of various methods of carbon dots’ synthesis, which can be divided into “bottom-up” and “top-down” approaches, make it possible to obtain nanoparticles of various structures: with a graphene or amorphous core, or consisting of carbonized polymers [1–6]. The sources of CDs’ luminescence can be the carbon core of nanoparticles (due to the quantum confinement effect), surface states and defects in the cores, and/or individual molecular luminophores, including those attached to the CDs’ carbon frame [1–3]. The maximum luminescence of such CDs can lie in a wide spectral range from blue to red [1–9]. Additionally, the photoluminescence of most carbon dots is sensitive to the changes in environmental parameters, such as the type of solvent, pH, temperature, and the presence of various ions [10–14]. The photoluminescence of CDs, combined with their nontoxicity, biocompatibility, chemical stability, simplicity and variability of their synthesis, ensures the active development of their applications in the fields of nanosensors, optoelectronics, and biomedicine [3,12–18].

Due to its simplicity, low cost, and eco-friendliness, hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots is one of the most common methods for the “bottom-up” synthesis of carbon dots – the formation of large particles from small precursor molecules. This method makes it possible to synthesize CDs with significantly different properties, primarily due to the diversity of possible precursors [19,20]. To carry out hydrothermal synthesis, an aqueous solution of precursors is kept at a constant temperature above 100 °C for several hours. As a result of the reaction between precursors and water, nanoparticles are synthesized, the properties of which depend not only on the choice of precursors, but also on the synthesis parameters used: temperature and duration of the reaction [10,21]. In the chemical reactions that occur in the synthesis of carbon dots, several processes are usually distinguished: dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation by organic molecules [22–26]. It is assumed that the initial reaction products are first transformed from individual precursor molecules into bulk polymers, which are then compresses with the gradual nucleation of aromatic crystallization centers, around which the carbon core of nanoparticles then grows with simultaneous passivation by various functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, etc.) or whole molecules. Depending on the duration of the synthesis, CDs of different nature are obtained: carbonized polymer dots or carbon nanodots [1].

Almost any carbon-containing substances can serve as precursors for the synthesis of carbon dots by the hydrothermal method, including natural organic matter (oranges, tomatoes, tobacco, bananas, etc.), biological waste (peels of fruits and vegetables, shells of nuts, steeped tea leaves, etc.) [27]. The chemical product obtained from such precursors requires careful purification, and the luminescence quantum yield of such CDs is usually in the range of 10–30 % [27]. At the same time, the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots from pure chemical substances produces a more homogeneous product; by changing the precursors, such complete control of the composition of the mixture makes it possible to achieve the synthesis of CDs of a certain atomic composition with a quantum yield of luminescence reaching 70–90 % [28–30].

Among the various precursors for the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs, citric acid (CA) stands out as a source of carbon: it is a ready-made carbon chain covered with chemically active carboxyl groups. The addition of nitrogen-containing precursors to CA leads to the synthesis of carbon dots doped with nitrogen, which significantly improves the photoluminescence properties of CDs including the luminescence quantum yield (QY) [31–32]. Among the co-precursors of CA, ethylenediamine (EDA) occupies a special place: carbon dots synthesized from the EDA:CA mixture luminesce in the blue region and can have a QY of >90 % [29,30]. However, this QY value is not always achieved; it depends on the main parameters of the synthesis – the ratio of the amounts of precursors, temperature and duration of the synthesis of nanoparticles; and this dependence is not monotonic [30,33–35]. Unfortunately, in these papers the properties of CDs from EDA and CA were studied by changing only one of the synthesis parameters: either the ratio of the amounts of precursors, or the temperature, or the synthesis time. The results obtained allow us to confirm that in the process of synthesis of CDs from EDA and CA, different stages are observed, but do not allow one to clearly define/mark their boundaries in the three-dimensional space of the main parameters.

The current consensus is that CDs from EDA and CA are a polymeric structure, as indicated, among other things, by chromatography, dialysis, and TEM data [35–37]. However, at the moment its exact chemical formula has not been determined and it is unknown how it changes during the synthesis reaction. The same applies to the sources of CDs’ luminescence. There is a hypothesis that the luminescence of CDs is due to photoinduced charge transfer between the amide and carboxyl groups of the polymer chains of CDs [36]. However, recently in the literature the more commonly voiced hypothesis is that the sources of bright luminescence of CDs from EDA and CA are molecular luminophores – derivatives of citrazic acid [33,38], in particular, IPCA (5-oxo-1,2,3, 5-tetrahydroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid or 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid) [33,35,37]. The share of such luminophores in the total mass of CDs can be significant. For instance, a quantitative analysis of the composition of CDs using quantitative solid-state NMR allowed to estimate the amount of IPCA in CDs synthesized by the microwave-assisted reaction of EDA and CA: 18±2 wt.% [37]. Moreover, different luminophores can be present simultaneously in one CD sample. However, at the moment, the question remains unsolved: at what stage of the synthesis CDs’ luminophores are formed and lost and how exactly the synthesis products’ structure changes when the synthesis parameters change.

In this study, the array of EDA and CA aqueous solutions undergone the process of hydrothermal method of CDs synthesis, while the EDA:CA ratio of precursors, temperature and duration of the synthesis varied over wide ranges. The optical properties of the resulting 392 samples were studied using photoluminescence spectroscopy and optical absorption spectroscopy, and changes in the synthesis products’ structure were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy. As a result, synthesis parameters that corresponded to the boundaries of the synthesis stages were determined, it was found out how the composition of the synthesized products changes during each stage – with formation of separate molecular luminophores and then their partial destruction/integration in the more complex structure of CDs.

Citric acid (CA, monohydrate, Ruskhim, analytical grade) and ethylenediamine (EDA, produced by JSC "EKOS-1″, special purity grade) were used as precursors of carbon dots. Double-distilled deionized water (conductivity 0.055 µS/cm, Millipore Milli-Q water purification system) was used as a solvent.

Synthesis reaction. A large array of samples was synthesized using the hydrothermal method by varying the EDA:CA precursor ratio in the range of 0–20:1, temperature in the range of 80–200 °C, and reaction duration in the range of 0.5–6 h 49 samples were obtained from CA without EDA and 343 samples from EDA and CA (total 392 samples).

To prepare precursor mixtures, 10.53 g of CA and 0–67 mL of EDA were added to water so that the total volume of the mixtures was 500 mL. In this way, 8 mixtures of precursors were prepared with the same concentration of citric acid 0.1 M and varying concentrations of ethylenediamine in the range 0–2 M, which provided a set of ratios of EDA:CA concentrations of 0/0.1/0.5/1/2/4/10/20:1. The resulting mixtures were poured into 10 mL Teflon containers, which were then placed in steel autoclaves. Autoclaves with a mixture of precursors were placed in a Sputnik muffle furnace (Russia) and kept for the duration of 30 min or 1–6 h in increments of 1 hour (7 samples in a series) at a temperature of 80–200 °C in increments of 20 °C (7 series in temperature). The synthesis time began to count from the moment the furnace temperature reached the required temperature; the heating rate was ∼25 °C/min. After the synthesis time had elapsed, the autoclaves were removed from the furnace and then naturally cooled to room temperature. All samples synthesized in this way were passed through a membrane filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm to remove fractions of large particles. It is worth noting that such filtration did not lead to a change in the optical properties of the carbon dots. In parallel experiments, the samples were also dialyzed against double-distilled deionized water through 1000 Da pores for 24 h The optical properties of the dialysate were almost indistinguishable from the properties of the unfiltered samples studied in this paper.

The synthesis of some samples was repeated 3–5 times. The results of the synthesis under identical conditions were qualitatively repeated, the average deviation of the quantitative characteristics of the optical parameters (see below) had the same order as the statistical error of repeated measurements for the same sample.

Further in the text, samples will be referenced according to the parameters of their synthesis: EDA:CA X:1, Y °C, Z h. It should be kept in mind that while at the studied processes the carbon dots (CDs, in the meaning of nanoparticles with carbon core and size greater than 2 nm) are formed, the products synthesized at certain parameter values are, rather, a set of molecular luminophores, partially combined into polymer chains [35,37].

The pH values of the studied solutions were measured using an Aquilon I-500 pH meter (Russia), equipped with METTLER TOLEDO InLab Nano pH electrode. The hydrodynamic dimensions and ζ-potentials of the reaction products in water were measured on a Malvern ZetaSizer Nano ZS device (UK) equipped with a laser with a wavelength of 633 nm; the signal was recorded at an angle of 173° Images of forming CDs were obtained using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using the annular dark-field imaging method of sample mapping of scanning electron microscopy (HAADF STEM) on a Tecnai Osiris microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The elemental composition of the dried samples was studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using spectrometers Axis Ultra DLD (Kratos, UK) and LAS-3000 (Riber, France), an aluminum anode (AlKα = 1486.7 eV) at a tube voltage of 12 kV and an emission current of 20 mA were used. C 1 s carbon and N 1 s nitrogen lines with binding energies of 285 and 400 eV, respectively, were used for calibration of the photoelectron peaks. IR absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a Bruker INVENIO R FTIR spectrometer (Germany). To do this, 15 μL of the solution was placed on a 2 × 2 mm diamond crystal of attenuated total reflectance (ATR) attachment and dried under a constant flow of room air for 10–15 min.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of aqueous solutions of the reaction products were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorimeter (Japan). Optical density spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Japan).

The PL spectra and optical density of the reaction products were recorded for the samples diluted with water to a state at which the optical density at a wavelength of 350 nm was ∼0.1. This dilution eliminated the inner filter effect (IFE) [40], which was caused by the absorption of both exciting and emitted samples’ light by the medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of samples’ photoluminescence signal parameters
ce:section.ce:para: For calculation of numerical parameters of the photoluminescence signal, from the obtained photoluminescence spectra the scattering bands at the unshifted frequency (Rayleigh), the second-order scattering band at the unshifted frequency, and the Raman valence band of water were subtracted using the SpectraGryph software package [39]. In addition, areas of intense noise signal caused by the low sensitivity of the detector at the boundaries of the measured spectral regions were nulled in the absence of a luminescence signal. For examples of 3D luminescence maps before and after processing, see Fig. SI-1.

2.3.1. The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the reaction products in aqueous solutions was determined by the standard dye method [40] using the formula

where Q is the QY of the sample, OD is the optical density, I is the integrated PL intensity, n is the refractive index of the medium; all parameters were determined for a wavelength λexc = 350 nm. The “r” index corresponds to similar parameters of the reference dye, which was quinine sulfate in an aqueous solution of sulfuric acid (H2SO4 concentration was 0.05 M). The value used for Q r was 58 %, determined for a wavelength of 350 nm [40].

2.3.2. To estimate the values of the integral luminescence intensity of the luminophores of the undiluted samples, after excluding the influence of IFE, the parameter luminophore intensity Ilumph was introduced:

Photographs of the synthesized mixtures at different ratios of EDA:CA, temperatures and reaction durations are presented in Figs. 1 a and SI-2.

The carbon dots synthesized in the studied process, as can be seen from the TEM image of EDA:CA 2:1, 180 °C, 6 h (Fig. 1b) or others (Fig. SI-3), do not have an obvious crystal lattice, which indicates their polymer structure [35,37]. However, it is worth noting that the nanoparticles distinguished by the TEM method are few and are sparsely distributed on the substrate. This is due to the fact that the main fraction in the studied samples has dimensions of about 1 nm (see below for a description of the hydrodynamic sizes and Fig. 1d) and, apparently, is not contrasting in TEM experiments. The characteristic size of single nanoparticles in samples EDA:CA 2:1, 180 °C, 6 h, EDA:CA 2:1, 200 °C, 3 h and EDA:CA 20:1, 140 °C, 3 h, according to TEM data, is 2–15 nm. At the same time, no particles were detected for the EDA:CA 0.1:1, 120 °C, 2 h and EDA:CA 20:1, 80 °C, 0.5 h samples. The pH values of aqueous solutions of the synthesized samples changed monotonically with an increase in the amount of EDA in the precursors from values of 2.1–2.5 for the samples without EDA to values of 11.8–12.1 for the samples from EDA:CA 20:1. Diluting samples with water by a factor of 103–104 to measure optical absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra without the influence of inner filter effect (see Section 2.2) led to neutralization of the solutions: pH values were brought to values of the order of 6.2–6.9. As follows from the results of the authors of papers [10,11,41], changing the pH of aqueous solutions of different carbon nanoparticles in the range of 5–8.5 does not lead to significant changes in their optical properties. Therefore, the discovered differences in the optical properties of the samples described below are mainly due to reasons not related to changes in pH.

The zeta potentials of synthesis products in water with the dominance of carboxyl groups of precursors (synthesis at an EDA:CA ratio of 1:1 and less) had average values from −5 to 5 mV, while samples with the dominance of amino groups of precursors (synthesis at an EDA:CA ratio of 2:1 and more) – have lower values from −10 to −25 mV (examples in Fig. 1c). At the same time, the hydrodynamic sizes of the main fraction of all synthesis products, regardless of the ratio of precursors, had average values of 0.7–0.8 nm; other fractions in some samples also had sizes of the order of 10–20 nm (Fig. 1d). Note that in TEM images (Fig. 1b) particles up to 1 nm in size are almost impossible to recognize, and therefore only larger fractions – carbon dots – were observed.

Fig. 1e shows the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of EDA:CA 1:1, 120 °C, 3 h Comparison of the resulting distribution of the number of C/O/N atoms in the sample with that in a mixture of its’ precursors – a mixture of EDA and CA molecules 1:1 – is of interest (Table 1 ). It indicates that the relative share of oxygen atoms in the sample's molecules significantly decreases during synthesis. If we assume that the number of carbon atoms in the reaction product remains constant, then as a result of the synthesis reaction of EDA:CA 1:1, 120 °C, 3 h ∼75 % of O atoms and ∼10 % of N atoms of precursors were “lost”. Such a decrease in the number of atoms corresponds to a loss of ∼37 % of the mass of precursors, which is in good agreement with the change in the mass of precursors in the result of the synthesis, determined by evaporation and weighing of a controlled volume of samples (see p. SI.1. Estimation of the products’ yields, Fig. SI-4). In addition to the comparison with the distribution of atoms in the precursors, the distribution of C/O/N atoms in the sample was compared with the distribution in the supposed main luminophore, forming during the CDs synthesis from EDA and CA – the IPCA molecule [33,35,37]. From Table 1 it is evident that the IPCA molecule contains 1 less O atom and 1 more C atom than in the approximate distribution of atoms in the EDA:CA 1:1, 120 °C, 3 h sample. The most probable cause of oxygen loss is the destruction of the carboxyl group of IPCA, the destruction of which is also follows from the IR spectroscopy data (see below). Thus, it can be concluded that if IPCA molecules are present in the samples’ structure, they are incorporated into the more complex polymer structure through the transformation of carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence in the samples from EDA and CA
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesized samples differed significantly in their color (see Fig. SI-2), absorption, and photoluminescence intensity. To obtain the photoluminescence spectra of the samples without the influence of IFE, as was noted above, most samples were diluted with water, the degree of dilution (DD) reached 8001.

Typical 3D PL excitation/emission matrices (EEM) of samples synthesized from EDA and CA at various synthesis parameters (100 °C < temperature < 180 °C and 1 h < synthesis time < 5 h) look similar to PL EEM of the EDA:CA 1:1, 120 °C, 3 h shown in Fig. 2 . Such photoluminescence EEM have two obvious peaks, the maxima of which are located in the λex/λem 245/440 and 350/440 nm/nm regions, with the intensity of the first being ∼80 % of the intensity of the second. The shape of the PL spectra does not undergo significant changes when excitation wavelengths change in the ranges of 220–270 and 300–400 nm, i.e., in areas where the luminescence intensity is high.

For samples synthesized in the absence of ethylenediamine or at minimum and maximum synthesis parameters from the used ranges (< 100 °C, < 1 h and > 180 °C, > 5 h), other PL EEM are observed (Fig. SI-5). In addition to low-intensity luminescence maxima in the regions of λex/λem 245/440 and 350/440 nm/nm, PL maxima of much lower intensity become noticeable, the position of the maxima of which for different samples corresponds to λex/λem 215/440, 265/450, 285/365, 280/325, 290/500, 320/395 nm/nm and/or 410/530 nm/nm(Fig. SI-5).

For the study of the EDA:CA products’ main luminophore, optical density spectra and photoluminescence spectra upon excitation at the wavelength of the main PL maximum of 350 nm were obtained for all samples (Fig. 3 , see Fig. SI-6 for all spectra).

Fig. 3b shows that the PL spectra of all samples excited at 350 nm are similar: they all have one luminescence band maximum, though located for different samples in the wavelength range from 410 to 490 nm. The structure of the absorption spectra of different samples is different: in addition to the main maximum observed for most samples in the region of 350 nm, for some of them bands with maxima in the region of 240, 285, 300, 450 nm are also observed, and the position of the main maximum can shift to 370 nm (Fig. 3a). However, samples with the most intense luminescence have luminescence spectra with a maximum in the region of 440 nm and absorption spectra with a maximum in the region of 350 nm. Samples that have spectra with different luminescence and absorption intensity maxima are also characterized by a significantly lower luminescence intensity. Such changes in the shape of the spectra indicate that the luminescence of synthesized EDA:CA structures is due to several luminophores (or several selected optical transitions), and the most intense luminescence of samples with different ratios of EDA and CA precursors is provided by the same luminophore with a maximum emission at λex/λem = 350/440 nm/nm. We denote this luminophore L350, since in the future it will be needed to explain the change in the optical properties of the products of the hydrothermal reaction at various stages of the synthesis known as the synthesis of carbon dots. Note that a detailed consideration of the multiplicity of luminophores that form in the hydrothermal reaction of EDA and CA is not the purpose of this article and will be presented in our other papers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and luminophore intensity at excitation 350 nm
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescence quantum yield is an important and most popular parameter for comparing the photoluminescence properties of fluorescent dyes and nanoparticles. But it is not the only factor that determines their optimal use in various fields: the PL intensity of the samples, associated, among other things, with the number of luminophores per volume unit, is also of interest on its own. As will be shown below, the PL intensity of the EDA:CA samples with the same QY, synthesized from the same mixture of precursors, but at different temperatures and synthesis durations, can differ by orders of magnitude.

The PL intensity values of synthesized EDA:CA structures, due to their high concentration in solutions, can be significantly distorted as a result of the influence of the inner filter effect. As mentioned earlier, to eliminate this influence, the samples were diluted to absorbance values at a wavelength of 350 nm about 0.1. The calculated integral intensity of photoluminescence of diluted samples, multiplied by the degree of dilution (DD) – the introduced parameter luminophore intensity, I
lumph (Formula 2) – determines precisely the integral intensity of luminescence of luminophores of undiluted samples for the case when there is no IFE. Thus, this parameter can be used to assess the efficiency of the synthesis of luminescent particles: it turns out to be proportional to the product of the QY and the amount of synthesized luminescent substance Clumph , obtained from the same number of precursors: Ilumph∝QY*Clumph . Note that for a correct comparison of I
lumph for the samples synthesized from a mixture of precursors with different ratios of EDA and CA, it is necessary to take into account the different concentrations of precursor atoms in them.

Based on the optical density spectra (Figs. 3a and SI-6a), PL spectra upon excitation at a wavelength of 350 nm (Figs. 3b and SI-6b) and information on the degree of samples’ dilution for all synthesized EDA:CA samples, the values of quantum yield (Formula 1) and luminophore intensity (Formula 2) at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm were calculated. A graphical representation of the obtained values of QY and I
lumph is presented in Fig. 4 , see their numerical values in Fig. SI-7.

Analysis of the quantum yield and luminophore intensity of EDA:CA samples at a wavelength of 350 nm (Figs. 4 and SI-7) suggests that the changes in the synthesis temperature (essentially the reaction rate) and in the synthesis duration are in some sense “interchangeable” in the main range of variable parameters (100–200 °C, 1–6 h). With an increase in the synthesis temperature by 20 °C without changing the reaction time and with an increase in the synthesis duration by 1.5 h without changing the temperature, QY and I
lumph undergo approximately the same changes. This conclusion will be confirmed by the results of IR absorption spectroscopy of the synthesized samples (see Section 3.4). It is interesting that such “interchangeability” of increasing the temperature and synthesis time is not characteristic of the synthesis methods for all CDs: as example, for CDs from citric acid and cyanamide, with an increase in the synthesis temperature of 140–240 °C in an open vessel, the luminescence color changed from blue to cyan to green, while changing the synthesis time from 1 to 10 h only slightly affected the position of the PL maximum [42]. From a comparison of the calculated values of QY and I
lumph of samples with different EDA:CA ratios, it is clear that the synthesis of brightly luminescent particles requires the presence of EDA as a precursor. In this case, the maximum QY value of ∼100 % can be achieved for the samples obtained with any EDA:CA ratio in the studied range of 2–20:1. However, the widest range temperatures and synthesis durations at which samples with a QY of ∼100 % are synthesized is observed with an EDA:CA precursor ratio of 2:1. At the same time, the highest luminophore intensity of samples (but with slightly lower, on average, QY values) is achieved for EDA:CA precursor ratio of 4:1. With an excessive amount of EDA in the precursor mixture (EDA:CA 10:1, 20:1), it is still possible to synthesize samples with a QY of ∼100 %, but the maximum luminophore intensity for them reaches only about 20 % of that possible with EDA:CA 4:1.

It is interesting that CDs with high QY values can also be obtained in cases where with CA other nitrogen-containing precursors are used instead of EDA. For examples, CDs synthesized from CA and urea in toluene reach QY of ∼92 % [43], CDs from CA and cyanamide have a QY of up to 73 % [42], and for CDs from polypropylene carbonate and EDA upon addition of CA the QY increases from 30 % to 86 % [44]. The PL spectra and the proposed luminescence mechanisms in them, however, are different: CDs from CA and EDA, synthesis of which is studied in this paper, have blue luminescence with a maximum in the region of 450 nm – its source is molecular luminophores [33,35,37,45] and, at later stages of synthesis, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [45]; yellow luminescence at 553 nm of CDs from CA and urea in toluene is due to conjugated sp2-carbon domains (fused rings) with edge groups [43], green luminescence at 521 nm of CDs from CA and cyanamide is due to nitrogen-doped graphitic carbon core surface-functionalized with abundant molecular fluorophores [42].

The fact that the luminescence of many highly efficient CDs is caused by molecular luminophores, the structure of which differs from the structure of the carbon framework of the CDs, confirm the need to introduce the concept of luminophore intensity, i.e., when synthesizing different luminescent particles, it is important to compare not only their QY, but also the value of the integral luminescence intensity of their luminophores, the concentration of which for different CDs may differ significantly. Samples with the highest QY may not correspond to samples with the largest number of luminophores and, accordingly, the highest intensity of CDs luminescence. Like that, Fig. 4 shows that the maximum quantum yield of luminescence of samples synthesized with a certain ratio of EDA:CA is achieved at a lower temperature/shorter reaction duration than the maximum I
lumph . That is, the brightest samples and samples with the highest QY are different samples. Moreover, the continuation of the synthesis reaction after reaching the maxima of QY and I
lumph leads to a decrease in the values of both parameters. The discovered discrepancy in the values of the maximum QY and luminophores intensity of samples synthesized from the same precursor solution indicates different processes and transformations of the structure of nanomaterials that occur during the studied synthesis of carbon dots. The study of these processes was carried out using IR absorption spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IR absorption spectroscopy of EDA:CA samples
ce:section.ce:para: As indicated in the Introduction section, in the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots, several chemical reactions are usually distinguished: dehydration, polymerization, carbonization and passivation by organic molecules. A detailed study of the structural transformations of substances occurring at different stages during the synthesis of carbon dots was carried out based on an analysis of changes in the IR absorption spectra of the initial, intermediate and final synthesis products.

Changes in the IR absorption spectra of the synthesis products occur even before the start of the synthesis itself – at the stage of mixing the precursors (Fig. 5 ). Note, that the turning point for many spectral changes is the transition from the concentration ratio of EDA:CA 1:1 to 2:1. EDA contains 2 amino groups -NH2, CA – 3 carboxyl groups -COOH, i.e., a change in the EDA:CA ratio from 1:1 to 2:1 crosses the 1.5:1 boundary when the amounts of -NH2 and -COOH are the same. That is, when the EDA:CA ratio is 1:1 and lower, there is a lack of amino groups of precursors relative to carboxyl ones, and when the EDA:CA ratio is 2:1 and higher there is their excess.

The observed changes in the IR absorption spectra upon mixing precursors (Fig. 5) indicate that during the interaction of EDA and CA nitrogen from EDA replaces the carboxyl groups of CA, as a result of which the vibration bands of carboxyl -COOH and amino groups -NH2 disappear, and bands of vibrations carbonyl group C=O in amides and nitro -NO2 groups appear. One should note the complete disappearance of vibrations of carboxyl C=O bonds at the time when the number of amino groups of precursors begins to prevail over the number of carboxyl groups: at EDA:CA 2:1. This disappearance is accompanied by the formation of an amide group between EDA and CA, most likely – through the formation of peptide bonds or the synthesis of citrazic acid derivatives [38] with a transformed carboxyl group.

It is also worth noting that the bands of stretching vibrations of CH2 and NH2 groups characteristic of EDA begin to appear in the spectra of precursors only with an excess of EDA – over the ratio of precursors EDA:CA 4:1. Moreover, even with an EDA:CA ratio of 20:1, these bands remain weakly expressed. This may be due to the fact that excess unreacted EDA molecules evaporate along with the evaporation of water when preparing the sample for the FTIR measurements, and the observed bands belong to only partially incorporated molecules, when one end of the molecule is bound to the polymer and the other end remains in the original state. This evaporation of ethylenediamine is consistent with a decrease in the mass of EDA:CA 20:1, 140 °C, 3 h by ∼73 % compared to the mass of the precursors (see p. SI.1. Estimation of the products’ yields).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural transformations in reaction products during the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrothermal synthesis – holding the mixtures of precursors at a temperature of 80–200 °C for 0.5–6 h in an autoclave – leads to changes in the structure of molecular bonds in solutions. From Fig. 6 , which shows the IR absorption spectra of all synthesized samples, and from Fig. SI-8, where the same spectra are divided into series with different ratios of precursors, it is clear that the greatest difference in the spectra is observed for samples from different ratios of EDA:CA, while different times and temperatures of synthesis introduce smaller variations. Moreover, a detailed analysis of the entire array of IR absorption spectra showed that, similar to changes in the quantum yield and luminophore intensity, changes in the intensities of IR absorption bands are maximum when comparing samples on the main diagonals of the “parameter – temperature – synthesis duration” matrices, while the difference in spectra on the side diagonals of the matrices is minimal (see the intensities of the C=O, C=C, -(C=O)-N, -N=O and -NO2 bands in Fig. 4). This result confirms the conclusion about the “interchangeability” of changes in temperature and time of the synthesis reaction, made on the basis of changes in QY and I
lumph (Section 3.3): an increase in the reaction temperature at the same duration is equivalent to its lengthening at the same synthesis temperature; the greatest changes in both optical properties and IR absorption spectra are observed with a simultaneous increase in both temperature and reaction duration.

The main changes in the IR spectra of samples during the synthesis of carbon dots can be seen in Fig. 7 , which shows the IR absorption spectra of mixtures of precursors and of samples synthesized from them at minimum (80 °C, 0.5 h), average (140 °C, 3 h) and maximum (200 °C, 6 h) temperatures and synthesis durations from the used ranges of their change.

Synthesis at minimal parameters (80 °C, 0.5 h) almost does not change the chemical structure of the samples: the spectra of the precursors and products of such a reaction are almost identical (Fig. 7). The same samples, according to Fig. 4, have minimal luminophore intensity and low quantum yield.

During synthesis with average parameters (140 °C, 3 h), significant changes in the spectra relative to synthesis at 80 °C, 0.5 h are observed only for samples with EDA:CA ratios of 0.5/1/2:1, while with a significant excess of both EDA or CA changes in the spectra are small. In the IR absorption spectra of samples with EDA:CA ratios of 0.5:1 and 1:1 with this continuation of synthesis, a significant increase in intensity and a shift towards lower wave numbers of the C=O stretching vibration band with a maximum in the region of 1700 cm-1 is observed, as well as decrease in the intensity of the C-O stretching vibration band in the region of 1180–1200 cm-1. Such changes indicate that the -O-H bond of the CA molecules that did not react with EDA when mixing precursors of carboxyl groups -COOH molecules in these mixtures is replaced, probably with the formation of longer polymer chains, while the C=O bond is still preserved. In the spectra of samples with EDA:CA ratios of 1:1 and 2:1, a new band appears in the region of 1645 cm-1, caused by stretching vibrations of C=C bonds [50,51] and indicating carbonization of precursors. In addition, in these spectra, with an increase in the EDA:CA ratio, an increase in the ratio of the intensity of the band with a maximum in the region of 1525–1565 cm-1 (ν(C=O)-N, νN=O, νNO2asym) to the intensity of the band with a maximum in the region of 1375 cm-1 (νNO2sym) is observed, which can be explained by the transformation of nitro groups -NO2 into nitroso groups -N=O with the loss of oxygen. Note that the slight increase in the absorption intensity in the region of 1675–1705 cm-1 for samples synthesized at temperatures and times greater than 140 °C and 2 h (Fig. 4) is not due to the appearance of C=O groups, but to an increase in the intensity of a broad band with a maximum at 1645 cm-1, corresponding to C=C bonds (see spectra in Fig. 7). It should be noted that samples synthesized at parameters (140 °C, 3 h) with average EDA:CA ratio have significant luminophore intensity, while for samples with a smaller amount of EDA, high values of I
lumph are not achieved at all, and for samples with a large amount of EDA, high values of I
lumph are achieved at high temperatures and synthesis times (Fig. 4).

In the IR absorption spectra of samples synthesized with maximum parameters (200 °C, 6 h), changes similar to those found in the spectra of previous samples (with average parameters) are observed. During a long-term synthesis reaction, in the spectra of samples obtained only from CA (with an EDA:CA ratio of 0:1), a decrease in the intensity of the νC–O band is observed, associated with the loss of -O-H bonds by the carboxyl groups of CA in the supposed polymerization process. The same process occurs for the samples with EDA:CA 0.1:1, 0.5:1 and 1:1, where not all -COOH groups are replaced by (C=O)-N or -NO2, during the synthesis at maximum parameters, as a result of which the remaining carboxyl groups are destroyed. This is evidenced by a more prominent decrease in the intensities of both bands of the carboxyl groups νC–O and νC=O, up to their complete disappearance in the spectra of samples obtained from precursors with an EDA:CA ratio of 1:1. In addition, in the IR absorption spectra of all samples with an EDA:CA ratio, starting from 0.5:1 and higher, the appearance and increase in the intensity of the νC=C band is observed, which indicates carbonization of the samples. This process is accompanied by a significant drop in the intensity of the νNO2sym band and a drop in the intensity of the ν(C=O)-N,NO2asym band. These changes can be explained by the fact that first, as a result of the loss of one oxygen atom, the nitro groups -NO2 transform into the nitroso groups -N=O, and then with the loss of the second oxygen atom, some of these groups completely disappear. It should also be noted that the described changes in the synthesized structures at this stage of synthesis are accompanied by a decrease in the QY and luminophore intensity of the samples: the number of luminophore centers in the samples reduces.
The changes in the intensities of the vibration bands νC=O, νC=C, ν(C=O)-N, νN=O and νNO2 for all samples described above in the form of matrices are presented in Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Features of the optical properties of EDA:CA polymerization products at various stages of CDs’ synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Above, we described the discovered dependences of optical properties (QY and luminophore intensity upon excitation at a wavelength of 350 nm) and structural transformations of reaction products in the process of hydrothermal synthesis known to produce carbon dots from EDA and CA on synthesis parameters – the ratio of precursors, temperature and duration of synthesis. All these parameters are presented in a single format convenient for comparison in Fig. 4.

Before a detailed analysis, one should note that the “surfaces” of equivalent results of synthesis at different values of temperature and synthesis duration appear in Fig. 4 in the form of arcs: increasing the synthesis duration leads to the same changes as increasing the synthesis temperature. This, in particular, suggests that in the used ranges of synthesis parameters, the reactions occurring with precursors’ molecules do not have obvious threshold values: these reactions simply accelerate with increasing temperature or take longer with increasing synthesis duration, but do not change fundamentally. On the map of synthesis parameters (Fig. 4), the continuation of the synthesis will mean a transition from one arc of equivalent synthesis results to another. In this regard, further analysis of the obtained characteristics of the results of the EDA and CA polymerization will be carried out for the generalized case of the synthesis reaction, and the specific temperatures and times of this reaction will be the values for samples located on the main diagonal of the matrices in Fig. 4.

Based on a comparative analysis of all the obtained characteristics (Figs. 4 and 5), the following explanation of the features of the optical properties of samples at each stage of the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from EDA and CA is proposed. Schematic representation of the occurring structure changes is shown in Fig. 8 .
1)
The initial stage of chemical transformations begins immediately upon mixing of precursors at room temperature (see Section 3.4.1). As a result of the interaction of citric acid molecules rich in carboxyl groups -COOH with the amino groups -NH2 of ethylenediamine, these groups are replaced by amide (-(C=O)-N) nitro- (-NO2) and possibly by nitroso- (-N=O) groups. Note that for mixtures with EDA:CA ratios of 0.1–1:1, this reaction does not occur completely: there is not enough EDA molecules in them to react with all of the carboxyl groups.
2)
The stage of the start of luminophores formation. Samples synthesized from any EDA:CA ratio at minimum synthesis parameters of 80 °C, 0.5 h already exhibit luminescence. This luminescence has a broad spectrum, the maximum of which shifts towards the red region with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. SI-5). However, the QY and luminophore intensity of such luminescence are low (Figs. 4 and SI-7). Moreover, continuation of the synthesis reaction leads to the destruction of this source of luminescence: QY and I
lumph drops even at synthesis for 1 h or at 100 °C.
In the presence of even a small amount of EDA in a mixture of precursors, during a synthesis equivalent to synthesis at 120 °C for 2 h, a detectable amount of luminophore L350 begins to form with an excitation maximum at a wavelength of 350 nm, whose spectrum shape remains unchanged when the excitation wavelength changes by ±50 nm (see paragraph 3.2). The QY of such a luminophore at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm is on the order of 100 %, while the lower values of QY at this wavelength are due to the overlap of the L350 absorption peak with the absorption of other components of synthesis products, which has a maximum in the shorter wavelength region (Fig. 3a).
In addition to this luminophore L350, luminescence caused by other luminophores is observed in the synthesized samples, for example see the luminescence of samples synthesized only from CA (Fig. SI-5). However, the quantum yield of such luminescence is quite low (not more than 10 %), and the amount of luminophores themselves is small compared to the amount of L350: their luminophore intensity I
lumph is at least 5 orders of magnitude less than the maximum achieved I
lumph caused by the L350 luminophore (Figs. 4 and SI-7).
For samples from EDA:CA 2:1 (having, on average, the highest QY), the synthesis parameters at which a sufficiently large amount of luminophores L350 are formed to achieve a maximum QY of the order of 100 % (but not the maximum luminophore intensity) are equivalent to the stage of synthesis of EDA:CA 2:1, 120 °C, 2 h A shortage of EDA or, on the contrary, its excess leads to a later achievement of the maximum QY of the samples. For example, for the samples from EDA:CA 0.5:1 and 20:1, these parameters are equivalent to that of EDA:CA 0.5:1, 140 °C, 3 h and EDA:CA 20:1, 160 °C, 4 h, respectively.
3)
Sample carbonization stage. Further hydrothermal treatment of the reaction products leads to a significant increase in luminophore intensity (Fig. 4), which indicates the continuation of the synthesis of luminophores, primarily L350 luminophores. But at the same time, the quantum yield of the samples begins to fall. Analysis of the optical density spectra shows that this QY fall occurs due to an increase in absorption at the studied excitation wavelength of 350 nm: in addition to a significant increase in the absorption of L350 luminophores, the absorption of other components of reaction products increases at this wavelength (example for samples from EDA:CA 2:1 see in Fig. SI-9). That is, at this stage, in addition to the synthesis of L350 luminophores, other active chemical transformations occur. An increase in the intensity of the IR absorption band at a frequency of 1645 cm-1, corresponding to vibrations of the C=C groups (Fig. 4), indicates that carbonization of the samples is actively occurring – corresponding to formation of CDs which starts to be found around that stage of the synthesis. This process is accompanied by the disappearance of nitro groups -NO2, which is evident from a decrease in the intensity of the IR absorption bands at 1375 cm-1, and an increase in the number of amide (C=O)-N or nitroso groups -N=O, which follows from an increase in IR absorption in the region of 1525–1565 cm-1 and a shift in the maximum of this band to shorter wavelengths (Fig. SI-8).
For samples from EDA:CA 4:1 (for which maximum values of luminophore intensity is reached), the parameters of the carbonization stage at which the maximum I
lumph is achieved, i.e., the maximum amount of luminophores L350 is formed (with the QY of the whole samples about 90 %), equivalent to the stage of synthesis of EDA:CA 4:1, 160 °C, 4 h Changing the amount of EDA relative to the 4:1 ratio in the original solution does not shift the optimal parameters for the degree of I
lumph , but reduces its value.
4)
Stage of decomposition of luminophores. Continuation of the hydrothermal treatment of EDA:CA solution leads to the decomposition of the synthesized luminophores L350: the luminophore intensity of the samples, as well as their QY, decreases (Fig. 4). At the same time, for samples made from EDA:CA 0.1–4:1, a decrease in the IR absorption of the C=C band is also observed (Fig. 4). Whereas for samples with an excessive amount of EDA, i.e., EDA:CA 10–20:1, an increase in the absorption of the C=C band is observed up to the maximum used parameters of 200 °C, 6 h. From the dependence of the intensity of the IR absorption band of C=C bonds for samples from EDA:CA 0–4:1 it is logical to assume that for samples from EDA:CA 10–20:1, the observed increase will also turn into a decrease with a further rise in temperature and reaction duration.
One should note that at this final stage of synthesis in the studied range of parameters, not only the IR absorption of the C=C bands decreases, but so does absorption of NO2 and (C=O)-N / N=O. The results of the studies [22–26] suggest that such a decrease in the intensity of the observed absorption bands is associated with the graphitization of the samples, with C=C bonds becoming part of aromatic rings, the formation of which also displaces nitrogen-containing groups, i.e. carbon cores of CDs are formed. The amount of luminophore L350 in such samples becomes significantly far from the maximum value; however, other less intense luminophores begin to appear in the spectra, for example, luminophores with a luminescence maximum in the region λex/λem = 320/395 nm/nm.

In this study, an array of mixtures of ethylenediamine and citric acid with EDA:CA ratio 0–20:1 undergone the hydrothermal method of CDs synthesis with the following parameters: temperature 80–200 °C and reaction duration 0.5–6 h. For all 392 samples, based on the data obtained via luminescence spectroscopy and optical absorption spectroscopy, the values of QY and luminophore intensity at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm were calculated. Using IR absorption spectroscopy, structural changes in EDA:CA polymerization products occurring during the synthesis process were studied, primarily the transformation of the carboxyl group -COOH, the formation of amide -(C=O)-N, nitro -NO2 and potentially nitroso -N=O groups, as well as the non-cyclic C=C bond were observed.

Simultaneous analysis of the results of optical spectroscopy and IR absorption spectroscopy made it possible to identify the main stages in the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots from EDA and CA and to establish that at these stages the temperature and time of synthesis are in a sense “interchangeable”: in the main range of parameter changes used (100–200 °C, 1–6 h) they can be perceived as the speed and time of movement along one path; the same changes in luminescence and structure of synthesis’ products can be achieved faster by using a higher temperature.

In the process of hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots from EDA and CA, several stages of synthesis were identified from the point of view of transformation of the structure of the reaction products and the formation of luminophores. 1) Polymerization of precursors begins immediately with their mixing at room temperature. 2) The stage of formation of luminophores begins already at minimal synthesis parameters: at 80 °C, 0.5 h, weak luminescence of the samples is observed. Increasing the temperature and duration of synthesis leads to a decrease in the initial luminescence and to the synthesis of the main highly efficient L350 luminophores with a maximum emission at λex/λem = 350/440 nm/nm with their own QY of the order of 100 %. An increase in the number of synthesized luminophores also leads to an increase in the QY of samples, reaching ∼100 % in the case of those from EDA:CA 2–20:1, when their absorption at 350 nm begins to dominate over the absorption of other parts of samples. 3) At temperatures above 120–140 °C and synthesis times greater than 2–3 h, the active synthesis of luminophores continues, but the carbonization stage also begins, leading to the formation of CDs. This process is accompanied by an increase in absorption at 350 nm, different from the absorption of the L350 luminophores, as a result of which the QY of the samples begins to decrease, despite the continuing increase in their luminophore intensity. 4) At a synthesis temperature and duration greater than 160 °C and 4 h, the stage of decomposition of luminophores begins: further carbonization of the samples’ compounds including L350 luminophores leads to graphitization – formation of carbon cores of CDs. This leads to both a decrease in the luminescence intensity and a decrease in the QY of the samples.

One should note that the synthesis of the main luminophore L350 occurs only in the presence of EDA in the precursors. Samples synthesized exclusively from CA have only very weak luminescence with a QY of the order of 2–4 %; noticeable synthesis of their own luminophore with a maximum in the region of λex/λem = 310/385 nm/nm occurs only at temperatures above 180 °C.

The maps of samples’ parameters obtained in this study in the three-dimensional space of the EDA:CA ratio, temperature and duration of synthesis, and the stages of CDs’ synthesis determined with their help, will make it possible to select the optimal parameters of EDA:CA synthesis for the specific applications of the reaction products as nanosensors, luminescent markers or theranostic agents. The results obtained provide optimization of the synthesis for the specified characteristics, for example, for the highest QY, for a large number of luminophores, for a high level of carbonization/dehydration of samples – formation of cores of CDs, or with a large number of certain surface groups. Thus, the results presented in the article are an important contribution to the development of “direct synthesis”.

Alexey M. Vervald: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Kirill A. Laptinskiy: Validation, Investigation. Maria Yu. Khmeleva: Investigation, Formal analysis. Tatiana A. Dolenko: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
